coredata.dc:title: Solid-state fluorescence based on nitrogen and calcium co-doped carbon quantum dots @ bioplastic composites for applications in optical displays and light-emitting diodes
coredata.dc:creator: Sharma, Varun Dutt
Kansay, Vishal
Chandan, G.
Bhatia, Anita
Kumar, Nikhil
Chakrabarti, S.
Bera, M.K.
coredata.dc:description: Biocompatibility, low toxicity, ease of functionalization, ecologically sustainable synthesis, and a diverse variety of applications have all contributed to the current popularity of fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a relatively new rising star among zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials. However, expensive precursors and production, as well as a time-consuming procedure, frequently restrict the economic design that must be addressed. In this work, we report an economical, sustainable and green synthesis of fluorescent CQDs co-doped with N and Ca from mustard (Brassica) flower extract without the need of additional doping element precursors. The quasi-circular or polygonal shaped CQDs were revealed to be yellow-greenish fluorescent in nature with outstanding photo-stability and excellent water solubility, with an average diameter of 4.38 nm and a relative quantum yield of about 15%. The compositional analysis, crystallinity, and functional groups contained in as-synthesized N,Ca-co-doped CQDs were determined by XPS, XRD, and FTIR analysis, respectively. The presence of dominant sp3 hybridized carbons over sp2 was confirmed by 1H NMR and 13C NMR spectroscopy. The plausible fluorescence mechanisms are also proposed. Additionally, a biodegradable bioplastic derived from corn starch was used to develop an N, Ca-co-doped CQDs@bioplastic composite in order to demonstrate how it may be used to produce down-conversion light emitting diodes and optical displays. The impact of CQD concentrations and pH sensitivity were investigated further.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Green synthesis
Bioplastic
Optical display
LED

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Green-synthesis of Brassica derived carbon quantum dots
Fabrication of quantum dot-based optical displays and down-conversion LEDs
Structural, morphological and chemical characteristics of N,Ca-co-doped CQDs
Optical absorption and emission properties of Brassica derived N, Ca-co-doped CQDs
Proposed fluorescence mechanism of Brassica derived N, Ca-co-doped CQDs
Applications of Brassica derived N,Ca-co-doped CQDs for quantum dot-based optical displays and LEDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The process flow of a simple, cost-effective, and eco-friendly synthesis of N,Ca-co-doped carbon quantum dots using mustard flower extract (Brassica) as a carbon source via hydrothermal method. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a)–(c) High-resolution TEM images, (d) d-spacing from HR-TEM images, (e) particle size histogram with curve fitted to the data using a Gaussian model, (f) SAED patterns and (g) energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) profile of as-synthesized N, Ca-co-doped CQDs derived from mustard (Brassica) flower extract. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) XPS survey spectrum of N, Ca-co-doped CQDs, High-resolution (b) C1s spectra, (c) N1s spectra, (d) O1s spectra and (e) Ca 2p spectra of N, Ca-co-doped CQDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) 13C NMR (b) 1H NMR spectra of N,Ca-co-doped-CQDs. (c) X-ray diffraction pattern (d) FT-IR spectra (e) Zeta potential of as-synthesized N,Ca-co-doped-CQDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) A comparison of UV–vis absorption spectra between CQDs synthesized with or without PEG. Effect of process (b) temperature and (c) process time variations on UV–vis spectra of N, Ca-co-doped CQDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) A comparison of fluorescence spectra of CQDs synthesized with and without PEG addition. (b–c) Fluorescence spectra of N, Ca-co-doped CQDs.subjected to (d) hydrothermal process temperature and (e) process time variations. (f) Digital photographs of as-synthesized CQDs at various process temperatures exposed to daylight and UV light (365 nm, 60 W). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) A comparison of normalized FL spectra of N, Ca-co-doped CQDs solution at different pH levels. (b)–(e) deconvoluted FL spectra of various pH dependent CQD solutions. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Excitation dependent FL spectra of as-synthesized N, Ca-co-doped CQDs. (b) corresponding contour plot between excitation v/s emission wavelength. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Schematic core-shell structure of synthesized N, Ca-co-doped CQDs. Proposed absorption and emission mechanisms for Brassica derived N,Ca-co-doped CQDs under different illuminations (b) 250 nm UV and (c) 400 nm blue light. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Digital photographs of various concentrated Brassica derived N, Ca-co-doped CQDs dispersed in DI water under (a) daylight and (b) UV illumination. Fabricated thin film on glass slide using N, Ca-co-doped CQDs@bioplastic composite that was irradiated with (c) daylight and (d) UV light. Fabricated quantum dot-based optical display with various CQD concentrations showing handwritten alphabet casting when lit with (e) daylight and (f) UV light. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Digital photographs of CQD solution at various pH levels under (a) daylight and (b) UV illumination. Fabricated thin film on glass slide using pH-dependent N, Ca-co-doped CQDs@bioplastic composite that was irradiated with (c) daylight and (d) UV light. Fabricated quantum dot-based optical display with various pH-dependent CQDs@bioplastic composite showing handwritten alphabet casting when lit with (e) daylight and (f) UV light. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

CQD concentration dependent emission (electroluminescent) spectra and corresponding CIE chromaticity coordinates according to CIE 1931 of the LEDs fabricated by integrating the UV-LED chip with N, Ca-co-doped CQDs@bioplastic composite phosphor derived from Brassica. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

pH sensitive emission (electroluminescent) spectra and corresponding CIE chromaticity coordinates according to CIE 1931 of the LEDs fabricated by integrating the UV-LED chip with N, Ca-co-doped CQDs@bioplastic composite phosphor derived from Brassica. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Solid-state fluorescence based on nitrogen and calcium co-doped carbon quantum dots @ bioplastic composites for applications in optical displays and light-emitting diodes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center for Hydrogen Fuel Cell Research, Korea Institute of Technology, Seoul, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center for Hydrogen Fuel Cell Research

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Biocompatibility, low toxicity, ease of functionalization, ecologically sustainable synthesis, and a diverse variety of applications have all contributed to the current popularity of fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a relatively new rising star among zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials. However, expensive precursors and production, as well as a time-consuming procedure, frequently restrict the economic design that must be addressed. In this work, we report an economical, sustainable and green synthesis of fluorescent CQDs co-doped with N and Ca from mustard (Brassica) flower extract without the need of additional doping element precursors. The quasi-circular or polygonal shaped CQDs were revealed to be yellow-greenish fluorescent in nature with outstanding photo-stability and excellent water solubility, with an average diameter of 4.38 nm and a relative quantum yield of about 15%. The compositional analysis, crystallinity, and functional groups contained in as-synthesized N,Ca-co-doped CQDs were determined by XPS, XRD, and FTIR analysis, respectively. The presence of dominant sp3 hybridized carbons over sp2 was confirmed by 1H NMR and 13C NMR spectroscopy. The plausible fluorescence mechanisms are also proposed. Additionally, a biodegradable bioplastic derived from corn starch was used to develop an N, Ca-co-doped CQDs@bioplastic composite in order to demonstrate how it may be used to produce down-conversion light emitting diodes and optical displays. The impact of CQD concentrations and pH sensitivity were investigated further.

ce:section.ce:para: We are living in the most inventive era in lighting since Thomas Edison introduced the light bulb. LED lighting technology has improved rapidly in recent decades. The LED lighting market is expected to be worth USD 75.3 billion in 2022, rising to USD 124.7 billion by 2027, at a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of 10.6% over the forecast period [1]. The thriving lighting business contributes the most to the increased demand for energy-efficient goods and LED-based improvements to current lighting products. Furthermore, technical advancements and increased environmental concerns have fuelled the expansion of the LED lighting industry. Other important reasons driving the growth of the LED lighting market are competitive production prices, strong economic growth rates, and large-scale industrial projects in the residential and commercial divisions. LED lighting has become commonplace in commercial and industrial settings, as well as schools, hospitals, and even residences. LEDs are extensively used in the sectors of lighting, backlighting, and display due to their low energy consumption, high light efficiency, long life, no radiation, and so on [2–7]. Therefore, it is critical to produce more affordable and environmentally friendly LED technology. Along with well-known light-emitting materials including organic dyes, rare-earth elements, and semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), carbon-based luminescent nanomaterials like carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are gaining prominence [8,9].

Since their unexpected discovery in early 2004, CQDs have demonstrated outstanding properties such as being environmentally friendly, easy to synthesize, non-blinking, high biocompatibility, electrochemiluminescence, durability, high photostability, extraordinary multi-photon excitation (up or down-conversion) capacity, ease of functionalization, and soluble in water with a high carbon content (up to 99.9%) [10,11]. CQDs are tiny nanoparticles (<10 nm) made up of sp2-or sp3-carbon domains that are rich in oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups. CQDs are usually spherical or quasispheroidal nanoparticles with varying degrees of crystallinity; they frequently exhibit excitation-dependent photoluminescence (PL).

So far, inorganic phosphors such as CdSe/ZnSe QDs, CdTe QDs, CdSe/CdS/ZnS QDs, CuInS QDs, CuInS2/ZnS QDs, InP/ZnS QDs, CdSe/ZnS QDs, CdSe/ZnS QDs, CdSe/ZnS QDs etc. have been utilized [12–18]. While QD-based phosphors have certain advantages in terms of light intensity, colour tunability, and energy consumption, the bulk of these QD phosphors contain hazardous elements including Cd, Te, and Se, which are harmful to the environment. Furthermore, because these phosphors are synthesized at high reaction temperatures using highly expensive precursors, their applicability in optoelectronics is limited. Down-conversion multicolor or white LEDs based on CQDs are an ideal choice in this circumstance. In the literature, many organic and inorganic precursor-derived CQDs for down-conversion multicolor or white LEDs have been described [19–28].

A simple, cost-effective, large-scale eco-friendly solution based on low-cost predecessors must, however, be conceived and manufactured. Natural biomaterial resources are typically preferred over other organic, inorganic, or synthetic resources in the search for an efficient, inexpensive, and environmentally friendly method of synthesis of CQDs because they are renewable and biocompatible, and they can convert biomass waste into worthy and valuable materials [29]. Besides, biomaterials extract has a complex variety of different plant metabolites, such as alkaloids, minerals, vitamins, glycosides, phenolics, terpenoids, and flavonoids, which may be useful for doping of various elements during CQD synthesis without the need for additional doping components. Furthermore, due to the negative environmental consequences of synthetic plastics, the development of biodegradable polymers for industrial and commercial applications is crucial today.

Synthetic polymers such as epoxy resin, PVK (poly-N-vinyl carbazole), silicone resin, or PMMA (Poly-methyl methacrylate) are now routinely utilized to embed CQDs in a solid state matrix for down-conversion LED applications. To the best of our knowledge, no investigations have been conducted on the effect of bioplastic as a solid phase dispersion matrix on the photoluminescent properties of CQDs.

Therefore, this study aims to develop an economical, sustainable and green synthesis of N, Ca-co-doped CQDs from a low-cost and nontoxic natural resource, mustard (Brassica) flower extract, as well as to investigate biodegradable corn-starch-based bioplastic as a solid phase dispersion matrix of CQDs for potential applications in quantum dot-based optical displays and down-conversion light emitting diodes.

Brassica flowers were picked from a local agricultural field on the university campus. The chemicals used in this investigation were all of analytical grade. All chemicals were used as received without any further purification. Other materials purchased from a commercial supplier included Whatman filter paper (grade 1), 0.22 μm syringe filter (polytetrafluoroethylene membrane), acetone, ethanol, polyethylene glycol (PEG) and isopropyl alcohol. Deionized water was obtained using a Milli-Q system (18.2 MΩ cm−1, Millipore, France).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Green-synthesis of Brassica derived carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: A cost-effective and facile one-step green synthesis route based on hydrothermal was used to synthesize carbon quantum dots from the flower extracts of Brassica. To extract the juice, the flower was mashed in a domestic mixer grinder. Following that, a 10:6 (v/v) ratio of 99.9% pure polyethylene glycol (PEG) solution was added with the fresh juice of Brassica plant extract. The solution mixture was then ultrasonicated for roughly 10 min before being put in a 50 ml stainless steel autoclave lined with Teflon. This hydrothermal process was repeated for 1–12 h at temperatures ranging from 110 to 220 °C. After cooling to room temperature, the as-synthesized solution produced was centrifuged for 15 min at 10000 rpm. The contaminants settled after centrifugation, and the supernatant solution was collected. To eliminate any remaining contaminants that were not removed by centrifugation, the supernatant solution was filtered using a 0.22 μm syringe filter (polytetrafluoroethylene membrane). Thereafter, the obtained purified solution was freeze-dried in lyophilizer under −40 °C and kept for later use. Stock colloidal solutions of CQDs in eppendorf were sealed in clear plastic bags to protect them from ambient moisture in order to increase their stability and simplicity of usage in device applications. The pH value of the as-synthesized was measured to be around 4.17. The complete procedure is depicted graphically in Fig. 1 .

The ultra-centrifugation of the sample was done with either REMI R8C or CPR-30 Plus centrifuge machine. The UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were recorded using a Molecular Devices SpectraMax iD3 spectrophotometer with a step of 1 nm in the wavelength ranges 230–700 nm and 250–700 nm, respectively. The stirring of the sample was done using a REMI MS-500 magnetic stirrer. The nature of surface functionalized CODs was assessed using a Shimadzu IR Spirit Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrophotometer in the wavelength range from 4000 to 400 cm−1. The X-ray Photoelectron spectra was measured by Physical Electronics PHI 5000 Versa Probe III ranging between 0 and 1200 eV. The crystalline nature of the CQDs was determined using a Bruker D8 Advance diffractometer at 40 kV, 40 mA and a non-monochromatic Cu K
α X-ray with an angular range (2θ) of 5-90o and an angular step of 0.02o. The nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopic (13C NMR and 1H NMR) measurements were carried out on a 500 MHz Bruker Advance Neo NMR spectrometer. Surface Zeta potential was measured by using Anton Paar Litesizer 500. The morphologies and selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns of CQDs were investigated using a JEOL JEM 2100 Plus high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The JEOL JEM-2100F's Energy-Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDX) was used to evaluate the relative concentration of carbon and oxygen in as-synthesized CQDs.

The relative quantum yield (QY) of the produced carbon quantum dots was estimated using the following formula:
φ =
φ r
(
I x
I r
)
(
η x 2
η r 2
)
(
A r
A x
)
, where x denotes the CQDs, r is used for the standard dye (quinine sulfate dye), I is related to the fluorescence emission intensity of the respective system, A refers to the optical absorption values, ɳ denotes the refractive index value of the material. The relative QY of CQDs was found to be around 15%. In addition, absolute QY was also measured which was estimated to be around 17.3%. Indeed, the higher quantum yield of CQDs obtained from various parts of medicinal/herbal plants is a source of concern, primarily because plant extract contains a medicinally diverse mixture of many plant metabolites, such as alkaloids, minerals, vitamins, glycosides, phenolics, terpenoids, and flavonoids, and improving QY remains difficult when compared to simpler inorganic or organic resources, even after careful processing. Supplementary Table S1 analyses the quantum yield of CQDs synthesized from various medicinal plants/herbs utilizing data from the literature in this context.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of quantum dot-based optical displays and down-conversion LEDs
ce:section.ce:para: A prototype of a quantum dot-based optical display was first fabricated using eco-friendly bioplastic materials. Commercially available corn starch, vinegar and glycerine was used to synthesize biodegradable bioplastic. Glycerine and vinegar with optimum volume ratio of 1:1 was mixed thoroughly with 0.2 gm of corn starch for about 10–15 min. After placing the solution on a hotplate at 50 °C for 15 min, various concentrations (5 mg/ml, 10 mg/ml, 20 mg/ml, 80 mg/ml) of as-synthesized N,Ca-co-doped CQDs solution were added dropwise until completely mixed and stirred for another 10–15 min. Before mixing, the CQD solution was well stirred in an ultrasonic bath for 5–10 min. The as-prepared CQDs@bioplastic composite was then coated on a glass slide using a doctored blade method. Similar casting procedures were used with CQD solutions of varying pH levels (pH: 2.54, 4.17, 7.4, 10.80). For alphabet casting, however, the as-prepared CQDs@bioplastic composite was put into a plastic cone and slightly compressed to create the letters. The casting design was then cured for 24 h at room temperature. Now to make the N,Ca-co-doped CQDs@bioplastic composite phosphor for LEDs, the mixture was put into a prefabricated dome-shaped plastic mould that covered a commercial ultraviolet (UV–C) LED (275 nm, 0.5 W) and left to rest at room temperature for 24–48 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural, morphological and chemical characteristics of N,Ca-co-doped CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphological characteristics of the as-synthesized N,Ca-co-doped CQDs were investigated by HRTEM analysis. The HRTEM images and corresponding selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns are shown in Fig. 2 a–f. The TEM micrographs (Fig. 2a–c) demonstrate that the as-synthesized N,Ca-co-doped CQDs are small, yet poorly scattered and agglomerated due to the lack of a passivation layer, and are quasi-circular or polygonal in shape. Meanwhile, the statistical distribution of CQDs was determined, and the associated histogram is presented in Fig. 2e, revealing that CQD sizes are largely scattered between 2 and 7 nm, with an average diameter of 4.38 nm, implying that CQDs have a high quantum confinement effect. It is apparent that the distributions exhibit normal distribution properties. We believe this is owing to the nearly polygonal/circular form of the quantum dots, the thickness of which is governed by the number of graphite layers. As a result, the TEM size distributions are the diameter distribution superimposed on the thickness (number of graphite layers) distribution. This also adds to the variability, as the distribution is affected by the orientation of the N, Ca-co-doped CQDs on the TEM grid. The crystalline nature of the N, Ca-co-doped CQDs is revealed in HRTEM images of the samples (Fig. 2d), and the lattice spacing was calculated using the fast Fourier transform (FFT). The measured lattice spacing of ∼2.58 Å is attributed to the (002) lattice plane of graphite [30,31]. Furthermore, the broad diffused ring in the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern revealed the as-synthesized N,Ca-co-doped CQDs' poor poly-crystalline structure (Fig. 2f). The elemental composition of the as-synthesized N, Ca-co-doped CQDs was investigated using energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), as shown in Fig. 2g. The presence of high carbon (at. percent 65.3) and oxygen (at. percent 25.6) concentrations, as well as tracer amounts of nitrogen (at. percent 4.7) and calcium (at. percent 2.4) concentrations, in energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy observations, suggest that the as-synthesized CQDs are effectively doped with N and Ca, but the presence of higher oxygen concentration reveals the possibility of presence of various functionalization groups (such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, amine, and carboxyl groups) on the surface of CQDs.

Several reports in the literature suggest that the optical properties of doped-CQDs are dependent on the chemical structure, such as surface functionalization, the extent of doping elements, and the position of dopant atoms (whether they are within the carbon core or on the surface) [31,31–33]. To study this X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed. The XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 3 a) reveals the presence of C, O, N, and Ca with binding energies of 284.6 eV, 531.8 eV, 399.5 eV, and 343.5 eV, respectively. The atomic percentages of C, O, N, and Ca were found to be approximately 63.2, 27.63, 5.7, and 3.48, respectively, which is nearly identical to the EDX analysis. We then deconvoluted high resolution C1s spectra into three components, each cantered at 284.6 eV, 285.9 eV, and 287.7 eV, representing sp2C-sp2C, N-sp2C, and C–OH bonds (see Fig. 3b) [33–36]. Similarly, the O1s spectra shown in Fig. 3c is found to be composed of two components with centres at 530.6 eV and 531.9 eV, indicating the presence of the C= O and C–OH groups [36–38]. The high-resolution N1s spectra (Fig. 3d) can only be deconvoluted into one peak, centred at 399.5 eV, which represents the presence of graphitic-N demonstrating that nitrogen atoms were effectively doped in CQDs [38,39]. Similarly, Ca 2p peaks (Fig. 3e) were deconvoluted into two peaks centred around 343.5 eV and 347.4 eV, which exhibit typical Ca 2p3/2 and Ca 2p1/2 peaks, respectively, confirming successful Ca doping in CQDs [40]. Therefore, the XPS analysis reveals that the economical and green synthesis of CQDs from the Brassica flower extract successfully doped with N and Ca without adding extra sources for doping.

Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy, on the other hand, was used to investigate the sp3-and sp2-composition of the core. Carbon quantum dots have been demonstrated to interact with sp3 and sp2–hybridized carbon atoms. The sp3 and sp2-hybridized carbon atoms were identified using 13C NMR. The 13C NMR spectra in Fig. 4 a clearly shows a combination of sp3 and sp2–hybridized carbon atoms, with sp3-hybridized carbon atoms predominating over sp2-hybridized carbon atoms. There were two distinct chemical shift (δ) zones observed, one in the range of 60–80 ppm with peaks cantered at 60.29, 69.39, 69.48,69.52,69.61,71.65 ppm related to sp3-carbons and carbons connected with hydroxyl and amine groups and the other peak at around 115 ppm and in the range of 180–200 ppm with peaks cantered at 184.15, 190.01, 200.51 ppm related to sp2-carbons and carbons connected with hydroxyl and amine groups. Meanwhile, the 1H NMR spectra shown in Fig. 4b show three distinct chemical shift (δ) regions: 1–3 ppm (dominated by sp3 C–H protons), 3–6 ppm (associated with hydroxyl group protons), and 8–8.5 ppm (for aromatic or sp2 protons) [40].

XRD validated the crystallinity of the as-synthesized N, Ca-co-doped CQDs, as illustrated in Fig. 4c. The obtained wide diffraction peak with a centre angle of 23.8° corresponds to the (002) plane, which was attributed to the interlayer packing of disordered carbon atoms in the hexagonal graphite structure of CQDs, resulting in a turbostratic structure [40–43]. Furthermore, the amorphous nature of the produced particles contributed to the wide peak. Meanwhile, the appearance of a stronger diffraction peak at 29.5° and a few faint diffraction peaks at 42.2°, 52.3°, and 61.2° correlates to the degree of graphitization along the (110), (200), and (211) planes (JCPDS Card No. 72–2091) [43]. However, the observed small peak at 43.4° is appearing from the Ca (220) plane. The interlayer distance (d-spacing) was calculated to be 0.37 nm for (002), 0.30 nm for (110), 0.21 nm for (200) and 0.17 nm for (211) planes.

As demonstrated in Fig. 4d, FTIR measurements indicated the existence of numerous functional groups and linkages in carbon quantum dots. Stretching frequencies at 3421.72, 2920.23, 1099.43, 1656.25, 2123.63, 1460.11 cm−1 strongly revealed the existence of –OH, –C–H, C–O, CO, C–N, and –Ca, respectively [44]. The carbon quantum dots synthesized are extremely water soluble due to the presence of these hydrophilic functional groups.

To acquire the surface charge, the zeta potential, also known as the electro kinetic potential of as-synthesized N, Ca-co-doped CQDs, was measured with a zetasizer. This approach also offers insight on the features of CQDs, such as double layer qualities with a range of hydrophilic or hydrophobic functional groups. The value of zeta potential is governed by the stability of CQDs particles during short or extended time periods. As illustrated in Fig. 4e, a zeta potential of −6.8 mV was obtained, demonstrating that CQDs are well stable in aqueous solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical absorption and emission properties of Brassica derived N, Ca-co-doped CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 5 a, the optical absorption peak of the as-synthesized N,Ca-co-doped CQDs without the addition of PEG was observed in the UV region, with a maximum absorption beginning about 327.3 nm and a tail continuing into the range of 428.7 nm. This is because of the normal π–π* transition of the conjugated CC band and the n–π* transitions in the graphitic core [43]. However, the maximum absorption peak was observed to be shifted to 337.6 nm for the PEG added as-synthesized N, Ca-co-doped CQDs, as shown in Fig. 5a, whilst the second absorption peak was identified at 484.9 nm. The creation of the second absorption peak is assumed to be caused by the presence of functional groups on the core's edge. This, in turn, will aid in the transfer of negatively charged ions from the heteroatom's non-bonding orbital to the core's π* orbital, resulting in n–π* transitions in prepared CQDs. Additionally, these absorption transitions between the core and edge states produce hole-electron excitons.

The influence of process variables such as temperature and time on as-synthesized N, Ca-co-doped CQDs using PEG was then explored further. The threshold absorption peaks were gradually pushed towards higher wavelengths, 412.4 nm for 110 °C, 425.7 nm for 150 °C, 436.2 nm for 180 °C, 440.6 nm for 200 °C, and 445.1 nm for 220 °C process temperature, while the process time for all was maintained constant at 3 Hrs (see Fig. 5b). As illustrated in Fig. 5c, a similar tendency was seen for process time variation while keeping the process temperature constant. It is probable that adequate agglomeration/nucleation occurs for a longer period of time, causing particle size to increase and, as a result, absorption peaks to move to higher wavelengths. As a whole, the hydrothermal process parameters were optimised to be 180 °C and 3 h based on consistency and repeatability, as well as UV–Vis and fluorescence properties. Furthermore, the conventional Tauc plot was used to measure the variation in optical energy band gap of the synthesized N,Ca-co-doped CQDs while hydrothermal process temperatures ranged from 110 to 220 °C [43]. According to the supplemental data, the calculated optical energy band gap is between 3.02 and 3.14 eV. (See Fig. S1 and Table S2).

Carbon quantum dots, on the other hand, are distinguished by their wavelength of emission and size-dependent fluorescence activity. Fig. 6 depicts the fluorescence (FL) spectra of N, Ca-co-doped CQDs. The fluorescence emission following excitation at 280 nm, as shown in Fig. 6a, was observed to be shifted towards lower wavelength for PEG passivated CQDs at the same concentration (∼1 mg/ml) of CQDs distributed in aqueous solutions (DI water). The deconvoluted fluorescence spectrum of PEG passivated CQDs sample revealed three separate wide peaks, with maximum emission peaks occurring at 413.4 nm, 451.5 nm, and 497 nm, respectively, with percentage contributions of 7.02%, 72.99%, and 19.97%, indicating the dominance of greenish-colour fluorescent emission. However, the deconvoluted spectra for CQDs without PEG, as shown in Fig. 6c, shows two wide peaks with maximum emission peaks located at 457.5 nm (93.26%) and 374.4 nm (93.26%) (6.73%). Meanwhile, the influence of process variables such as temperature and time on fluorescence emission from PEG-coated CQDs was investigated. The emission spectra broadened as the process temperature was raised, but the maximal emission peaks remained essentially the same at a wavelength (470 nm), as seen in Fig. 6d. The analysis details can be found from Supplementary Fig. S2. A similar pattern was seen for differences in process time (see Fig. 6e). It is expected that higher amounts of agglomeration/nucleation occur at higher temperatures and over a longer length of time, resulting in increased particle size and, as a result, wider FL emission peaks. The aqueous solutions of N, Ca-co-doped CQDs with or without PEG passivation were pale yellow and transparent in daytime, but when exposed to UV light (365 nm, 60 W), they fluorescence brightly and intensely green, as seen in the inset of Fig. 6f.

Another distinguishing feature of CQDs is pH sensitive fluorescence. As a result, the effect of pH on fluorescence properties was investigated in this work. The pH-dependent fluorescence of PEG passivated N, Ca-co-doped CQDs is shown in Fig. 7 a. It is worth noting that the emission peak broadens as the pH value increases from acid (pH:2.54) to base (pH:10.8). In fact, deconvolution of the measured FL spectra (Fig. 7b–e) shows that the peak maxima changes towards higher wavelength (458 nm for pH:2.54 vs. 469.6 nm for pH:10.8) when pH value goes towards base. It is most likely due to the protonation or deprotonation of an oxygenated functional group, which causes the emission wavelength to move upward. Meanwhile, the pH-dependent UV–Vis characteristics are shown in Supplementary Fig. S3.

The excitation dependent FL spectra, on the other hand, might be helpful in developing and implementing the down-conversion strategy for LEDs. As a result, excitation-dependent FL emission was investigated. When the excitation wavelength was increased from 250 to 520 nm, the emission wavelength increased from 359 to 583 nm (see Fig. 8 a). On Fig. 8b, colour-coded maps of photoemission are shown, with emission wavelength in the x-axis, excitation in the y-axis, and the colour scale showing the FL intensity. As the excitation wavelength increases, the fluorescence emission peaks move towards redshift, indicating photon reabsorption. The results show that CQDs contain excitation-dependent fluorescence, which is connected to the quantum-confinement effect and edge defects.

Besides, the aqueous solution of N, Ca-co-doped CQDs demonstrated a long-term homogeneous phase with no obvious precipitation at ambient temperature. This was verified further by employing the Tyndall effect, which is the most widely used approach for particle dispersibility in a colloid, and leveraging the phenomena of light scattering in colloidal dispersion while demonstrating no light in a real solution [45]. We utilized a red laser beam (650 nm, 5 mW) to illuminate the colloidal solution of as-synthesized N, Ca-co-doped CQDs with pH values ranging from 2.53 to 11.35. Individual suspended CQDs scatter and reflect light, allowing the laser beam to be seen traveling through colloidal solutions whereas non-colloidal DI water remains clear due to the Tyndall effect as shown in Supplementary Fig. S4. Besides, the stability of the suspended particles is generally measured by the amplitude of the zeta potential. As shown in Supplementary Fig. S5, the pH variation of zeta potential has been detected, with a considerable shift in positive directions when pH is more acidic (from −6.8 mV @ pH:4.17 to +3.6 mV @ pH:2.53). This implies that additional positive charge is formed, most likely as a result of the reversible protonation and deprotonation of functional groups such as carboxylic and amide groups. Despite the fact that there is no obvious distinction between the zeta value of CQDs and its relationship with stability, a sufficiently high zeta potential value from its null indicates the remarkable stability of as-synthesized CQDs in aqueous solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed fluorescence mechanism of Brassica derived N, Ca-co-doped CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots emit photoluminescence that varies with excitation wavelength. Electronic bandgap variations caused by conjugated -domains, surface defect states, local fluorophores, and heteroatom doping are commonly linked to fluorescence colour (such as nitrogen, calcium in this case). Based on the XPS, FTIR, and fluorescence spectra of Brassica derived N, Ca-co-doped CQDs, a scheme of energy band structure is proposed in this work, as shown in Fig. 9 . The observed excitation dependent FL emission spectra, particularly on the lower energy side, as shown in Fig. 8, clearly indicate the presence of various chromophoric band-gap structures created by groups related to C, N, and Ca, such as C–C/CC (intrinsic-C), C–N (graphitic-N), C–Ca, CO groups, or grain boundaries [46]. Surface defects, on the other hand, are created mostly by surface oxidation or dangling bonds and can serve as a capture site for excitons, resulting in surface defect state fluorescence. The optical band-gap structure of Brassica derived N, Ca-co-doped CQDs is proposed under UV (250 nm) and visible blue light (400 nm) illuminations, as shown in Fig. 9b and c, respectively. The absorption of UV photons by the localised π-electron in double bonds (mostly CC) under 250 nm UV excitation produces an electron-hole pair (i.e. exciton) during the electron transition from the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of carbon core, H1 → L1. After vibration relaxation through route-I (L1 →H1), this exciton may produce UV light via radiative recombination. The excited electron, on the other hand, may undergo an inter-band transition from a higher LUMO to a lower LUMO through the routes L1 →L2, L1 →L3 or L1 →L4 after which it generates visible light via the routes II (L2 →H2), III (L3 →H3) or IV (L4 →H4) (see Fig. 9b for details). When subjected to 400 nm visual stimulation, conjugated -electrons elevate to H2 →L2 after absorbing blue photons and are capable of transmitting excitons and producing visible light through route-I' (see Fig. 9c). During vibration relaxation, it may also take route-II’ (L3 →H3) or route-III’ (L4 →H4). As seen in Fig. 9c, such an excitation produces photons with broader emission wavelengths of approximately 496 nm, which are longer than the absorbed wavelengths (400 nm). Therefore, it is believed that the presence of N and Ca can boost the fluorescence of CQDs by self-passivation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of Brassica derived N,Ca-co-doped CQDs for quantum dot-based optical displays and LEDs
ce:section.ce:para: As previously stated, synthetic polymers such as epoxy resin, PVK (poly-N-vinyl carbazole), silicone resin, or PMMA (Poly-methyl methacrylate) are now commonly used to embed CQDs in a solid state matrix for down-conversion LED applications; however, the purpose of this study is to demonstrate a prototype of a quantum dot-based optical display as well as LEDs using an alternative, eco-friendly corn-starch-based bioplastic. The literature survey for materials utilized in down-conversion LED fabrication with CQDs is summarized in Table S3.

The bioplastic was chosen not only for its convenience of use, low cost, and environmental friendliness, but also to avoid self-quench in solid state due to aggregation. Meanwhile, the starch matrix does not compete with excitation light for absorption and does not absorb CQD emissions, hence boosting fluorescence efficiency.

The impact of CQD concentrations on optical displays was first examined. Fig. 10 a and b demonstrate digital pictures of as-synthesized N,Ca-co-doped CQDs at various concentrations (5 mg/ml, 10 mg/ml, 20 mg/ml, and 80 mg/ml) under daylight and UV irradiation (365 nm, 60 W), respectively. The emission colour varies from light green to dark yellowish green. Similar tendencies have also been observed for the CQDs@bioplastic composite thin film as well as letter casting on glass slides as shown in Fig. 10 c-f. CQD concentration dependent CIE (Commission Internationale de l'Eclairage) chromaticity coordinates were calculated using fluorescence spectra from CIE 1931 and 1976, as shown in Supplementary Fig. S6. Meanwhile, optical displays fabricated using N, Ca-co-doped CQDs at varied pH levels, on the other hand, have been studied. As previously found in FL spectra, the fabricated optical display, whether in thin film or letter casting format, visibly changes in colour from deep green to yellowish green while pH value increases from acid (pH:2.54) to base (pH:10.8) when subjected to UV irradiation, as illustrated in Fig. 11 a-f. The chromaticity coordinate details can be found from Supplementary Table S4.

In addition, N, Ca-co-doped CQDs@bioplastic were used to fabricate down-conversion LEDs, the specifics of which are provided in the experiment section. Initially, the effect of CQD concentration on LED conversion was explored. As shown in Fig. 12 , as the concentration increases from 10 mg/ml to 80 mg/ml, the emission colour of the converted UV-LED shifts to a higher wavelength (yellowish-green). The quality of the light produced, as well as the efficiency metrics of the converted UV LED, were also evaluated, as shown in Fig. 12. Fig. 12b show the electroluminescent (EL) spectrum of the converted LED at a forward bias current of 15 mA for various concentrations (10 mg/ml, 20 mg/ml, 80 mg/ml) CQDs@bioplastic samples. The chromatic coordinates of the various concentrated samples are (0.44, 0.49), (0.41, 0.49), and (0.42, 0.50) for 10 mg/ml, 20 mg/ml, and 80 mg/ml, respectively, according to the Commission Internationale de l'Eclairage (CIE-1931). Meanwhile, the emitted colour was investigated for CQDs at various pH levels but at a certain concentration (5 mg/ml), as shown in Fig. 13 . The CIE-1931 chromatic coordinates change slightly from (0.44, 0.49) to (0.44, 0.48) as the pH value increases from acid (pH:2.54) to base (pH:10.8), as seen in Fig. 13c. This demonstration clearly illustrates how N,Ca-co-doped CQDs@bioplastic composite may be implemented to provide a simple and cost-effective technique of down-converting UV LED chips into yellowish-green LEDs. However, future study might concentrate on the essential optical properties (such as CIE and efficiency) of CQD@bioplastic-based down-conversion LEDs.

To sum up, a simple, cost-effective, and eco sustainable synthesis approach of yellow-green fluorescence emitting carbon quantum dots derived from inexpensive mustard flower extract, Brassica is reported. As-synthesized CQDs were effectively doped with calcium and nitrogen due to the internal compositions of Brassica flower extract, despite the lack of additional doping element precursors. The as-synthesized N,Ca-co-doped CQDs were discovered to have a yellow-greenish fluorescent nature, strong photo-stability, and excellent water solubility. They were found to be predominantly dispersed in the range of 2–7 nm, with an average diameter of 4.38 nm, a relative quantum yield of about 15%, and to be quasi-circular or polygonal in shape. N, Ca-co-doped CQDs were synthesized, and their composition, crystallinity, and functional groups were evaluated by XPS, XRD, and FTIR studies. The presence of sp3 and sp2 carbons was confirmed by 1H NMR and 13C NMR spectroscopy. There is also discussion of potential fluorescence processes. On the other hand, the development of biodegradable plastics for industrial and commercial uses is crucial nowadays due to the negative environmental consequences of synthetic plastics. Thus, in this work, N,Ca-co-doped CQDs@bioplastic composite materials were produced using biodegradable plastic made from corn starch. Starch provides advantages due to the hydroxyl groups on its surfaces, which may readily absorb CQDs via hydrogen bonding. Furthermore, non-radiative decay processes and photoluminescence quenching caused by CQD agglomeration may be avoided by properly distributing CQDs on the surfaces of starch particles. Besides, the starch matrix does not compete for absorbing excitation light and does not absorb CQDs emissions, boosting fluorescence efficiency.

The N,Ca-co-doped CQDs@bioplastic composite was then successfully used to demonstrate the quantum dot-based optical displays and down-conversion light emitting diodes. Besides, the impacts of various CQD concentrations as well as pH sensitivity on the fluorescence properties were investigated utilizing fabricated quantum dot-based optical displays and down-conversion light emitting diodes using chromatic coordinates as defined by the Commission Internationale de l'Eclairage (CIE-1931). This study clearly demonstrates that N,Ca-co-doped CQDs@bioplastic composite has the potential to implement a facile, cost-effective and eco-sustainable technique of down-converting UV LED chips into yellowish-green LEDs.

Varun Dutt Sharma: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Vishal: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. G. Chandan: Investigation, Formal analysis. Anita Bhatia: Visualization. Nikhil Kumar: characterizations. S. Chakrabarti: Methodology, Supervision. M.K. Bera: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Writing – review & editing, overall Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
